questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Let $V$ be the volume of the ball and $\rho$ be its density.

The density of the liquid is given as $\rho_L = \dfrac{\rho}{\eta}$.

The forces acting on the ball in the liquid are its weight $W$ acting downwards and the buoyant force $F_B$ acting upwards.

$W = V \rho g$

$F_B = V \rho_L g = V \left(\dfrac{\rho}{\eta}\right) g$

The net downward force on the ball is:

$F_{\text{net}} = W - F_B = V \rho g - V \left(\dfrac{\rho}{\eta}\right) g = V \rho g \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\eta}\right)$

The effective acceleration due to gravity $g_{\text{eff}}$ is:

$g_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{net}}}{m} = \dfrac{V \rho g \left(\dfrac{\eta - 1}{\eta}\right)}{V \rho} = g \left(\dfrac{\eta - 1}{\eta}\right)$

The period of small oscillations of the pendulum is given by:

$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\ell}{g_{\text{eff}}}}$

Substituting the value of $g_{\text{eff}}$, we get:

$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\ell}{g \left(\dfrac{\eta - 1}{\eta}\right)}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\eta \ell}{(\eta - 1)g}}$

Answer: (2) $2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\eta \ell}{(\eta - 1)g}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The probability $dP$ of finding the particle in the interval from $x$ to $x + dx$ is equal to the fraction of time it spends in this interval during one complete oscillation.

Since the particle passes through the interval $(x, x + dx)$ twice in one time period $T$, the time spent in this interval is $2 dt$.

$dP = \dfrac{2 dt}{T}$

Given the equation of motion $x = a \cos \omega t$, the magnitude of velocity is:

$v = \left| \dfrac{dx}{dt} \right| = a \omega \sin \omega t = \omega \sqrt{a^2 - x^2}$

The time $dt$ taken to cover the distance $dx$ is:

$dt = \dfrac{dx}{v} = \dfrac{dx}{\omega \sqrt{a^2 - x^2}}$

Substituting $dt$ and $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ into the probability expression:

$dP = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}} \left( \dfrac{dx}{\omega \sqrt{a^2 - x^2}} \right)$

$dP = \dfrac{\omega}{\pi} \dfrac{dx}{\omega \sqrt{a^2 - x^2}} = \dfrac{dx}{\pi \sqrt{a^2 - x^2}}$

Therefore, the probability density is:

$\dfrac{dP}{dx} = \dfrac{1}{\pi \sqrt{a^2 - x^2}}$

Answer: (1) $\dfrac{1}{\pi \sqrt{a^2 - x^2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
options: The velocity projection $v_x$ as a function of $x$ is given by $v_x = \pm 2\omega \sqrt{x(a-x)}$.
The amplitude of oscillation is $a/2$ and the period is $\pi/\omega$.
The velocity projection $v_x$ as a function of $x$ is given by $v_x = \pm \omega \sqrt{x(a-x)}$.
The amplitude of oscillation is $a$ and the period is $2\pi/\omega$.
questions.solution:
Given $x = a \sin^2\left(\omega t - \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$

Using the identity $\sin^2 \theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$x = \dfrac{a}{2} \left[1 - \cos\left(2\omega t - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]$

$x = \dfrac{a}{2} - \dfrac{a}{2} \sin(2\omega t)$

This represents simple harmonic motion about the mean position $x = \dfrac{a}{2}$.

The amplitude of oscillation is $A = \dfrac{a}{2}$.

The angular frequency is $\omega' = 2\omega$, so the period is $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega'} = \dfrac{2\pi}{2\omega} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$.

Differentiating the given equation with respect to time $t$ to find velocity $v_x$:

$v_x = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = 2a\omega \sin\left(\omega t - \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) \cos\left(\omega t - \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$

From the given equation, $\sin\left(\omega t - \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) = \pm \sqrt{\dfrac{x}{a}}$

And $\cos\left(\omega t - \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) = \pm \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\left(\omega t - \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)} = \pm \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{x}{a}} = \pm \sqrt{\dfrac{a-x}{a}}$

Substituting these into the velocity expression:

$v_x = \pm 2a\omega \sqrt{\dfrac{x}{a}} \sqrt{\dfrac{a-x}{a}} = \pm 2\omega \sqrt{x(a-x)}$

Answer: (1, 2)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Let the liquid be displaced by a small distance $x$ along the length of the tube.

The level of the liquid in the vertical left arm falls by a vertical distance $x$.

The liquid in the right arm moves up along the tube by a distance $x$. Since the right arm is inclined at an angle $\theta$ with the vertical, the vertical rise of the liquid level in this arm is $x \cos \theta$.

The total difference in the vertical heights of the liquid levels in the two arms becomes:

$h = x + x \cos \theta = x(1 + \cos \theta)$

This difference in height creates an unbalanced liquid column which provides the restoring force. The restoring pressure difference is:

$\Delta P = \rho g h = \rho g x(1 + \cos \theta)$

The restoring force $F$ acting on the entire mass $m$ of the liquid is:

$F = - \Delta P \times S = - \rho g S (1 + \cos \theta) x$

Using Newton's second law, $F = ma$, we get:

$ma = - \rho g S (1 + \cos \theta) x$

$a = - \left( \dfrac{\rho g S (1 + \cos \theta)}{m} \right) x$

Comparing this with the standard equation of simple harmonic motion, $a = - \omega^2 x$, we find the angular frequency squared:

$\omega^2 = \dfrac{\rho g S (1 + \cos \theta)}{m}$

The time period of oscillation $T$ is given by $T = \dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}$:

$T = 2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{\rho g S (1 + \cos \theta)}}$

Answer: (4) $2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{\rho g S (1 + \cos \theta)}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
In the non-inertial frame of the point of suspension, the pendulum bob experiences a pseudo acceleration $-\vec{w}$ in addition to the acceleration due to gravity $\vec{g}$.

The effective acceleration due to gravity is given by:

$\vec{g}_{eff} = \vec{g} - \vec{w}$

The magnitude of the effective acceleration is:

$g_{eff} = |\vec{g} - \vec{w}| = \sqrt{g^2 + w^2 - 2gw \cos\beta}$

The period of small oscillations of the pendulum is:

$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\ell}{g_{eff}}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\ell}{(g^2 + w^2 - 2gw \cos\beta)^{1/2}}}$

Substituting the given values $\ell = 0.21$ m, $w = \dfrac{g}{2}$, and $\beta = 120^\circ$:

$g_{eff} = \sqrt{g^2 + \left(\dfrac{g}{2}\right)^2 - 2g\left(\dfrac{g}{2}\right)\cos 120^\circ}$

$g_{eff} = \sqrt{g^2 + \dfrac{g^2}{4} - g^2\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)} = \sqrt{g^2 + \dfrac{g^2}{4} + \dfrac{g^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{7g^2}{4}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{2}g$

Now, substituting $g_{eff}$ into the time period formula:

$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{0.21}{\dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{2}g}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{0.42}{\sqrt{7}g}}$

Taking $g = 9.8$ m/s$^2$:

$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{0.42}{9.8\sqrt{7}}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{70\sqrt{7}}} \approx 0.8$ s

Answer: (2) $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\ell}{(g^2 + w^2 - 2gw \cos\beta)^{1/2}}}$, $T = 0.8$ s

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The displacement of the plank is given by $y = a(1 - \cos \omega t)$.

The velocity of the plank is $v = \dfrac{dy}{dt} = a\omega \sin \omega t$.

The acceleration of the plank is $A = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = a\omega^2 \cos \omega t$.

Let $N$ be the normal reaction exerted by the plank on the body. The equation of motion for the body is $N - mg = mA$, which gives $N = m(g + a\omega^2 \cos \omega t)$.

The body starts falling behind the plank when it loses contact, which occurs when $N = 0$. This requires $g + a\omega^2 \cos \omega t = 0$, or $\cos \omega t = -\dfrac{g}{a\omega^2}$.

Since $\cos \omega t \ge -1$, the condition for the body to lose contact is $\dfrac{g}{a\omega^2} \le 1$, which gives $a \ge \dfrac{g}{\omega^2}$.

The minimum amplitude is $a_{min} = \dfrac{g}{\omega^2} = \dfrac{9.8}{11^2} = \dfrac{9.8}{121} \approx 0.081$ m $= 8.1$ cm.

At the moment the body loses contact, $\cos \omega t = -\dfrac{g}{a\omega^2}$. The height of the body at this instant is $y_0 = a(1 - \cos \omega t) = a + \dfrac{g}{\omega^2}$.

The velocity of the body at this instant is $v_0 = a\omega \sin \omega t = a\omega \sqrt{1 - \cos^2 \omega t} = a\omega \sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{g}{a\omega^2}\right)^2} = \sqrt{a^2\omega^2 - \dfrac{g^2}{\omega^2}}$.

After losing contact, the body moves under gravity as a free projectile. The maximum height reached above the point of losing contact is $h' = \dfrac{v_0^2}{2g} = \dfrac{a^2\omega^2}{2g} - \dfrac{g}{2\omega^2}$.

The total height reached by the body relative to the initial position is $h = y_0 + h' = a + \dfrac{g}{\omega^2} + \dfrac{a^2\omega^2}{2g} - \dfrac{g}{2\omega^2} = a + \dfrac{g}{2\omega^2} + \dfrac{a^2\omega^2}{2g}$.

Multiplying by $2g$, we get $2gh = 2ga + \dfrac{g^2}{\omega^2} + a^2\omega^2 = \left(a\omega + \dfrac{g}{\omega}\right)^2$.

Taking the square root, $a\omega + \dfrac{g}{\omega} = \sqrt{2gh}$, which gives $a = \dfrac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\omega} - \dfrac{g}{\omega^2}$.

Substituting $h = 0.5$ m, $g = 9.8$ m/s$^2$, and $\omega = 11$ s$^{-1}$:

$a = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 0.5}}{11} - \dfrac{9.8}{121} = \dfrac{\sqrt{9.8}}{11} - \dfrac{9.8}{121} \approx 0.2846 - 0.0810 = 0.2036$ m $\approx 20.4$ cm.

Answer: (1) (i) $8.1$ cm, (ii) $20.4$ cm

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The equations of motion for the particle in the $x$ and $y$ directions are given by:

$m\dfrac{d\dot{x}}{dt} = a\dot{y}$

$m\dfrac{d\dot{y}}{dt} = -a\dot{x}$

Integrating both equations with respect to time $t$, we get:

$m\dot{x} = ay + C_1$

$m\dot{y} = -ax + C_2$

At $t = 0$, the particle is at the origin ($x = 0, y = 0$) and has velocity $\vec{v} = v_0\hat{j}$, which means $\dot{x} = 0$ and $\dot{y} = v_0$. Substituting these initial conditions:

$0 = a(0) + C_1 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$

$mv_0 = -a(0) + C_2 \Rightarrow C_2 = mv_0$

Substituting the constants back into the velocity equations:

$\dot{x} = \dfrac{a}{m}y$

$\dot{y} = -\dfrac{a}{m}x + v_0$

To find the equation of the trajectory, we eliminate time by dividing the two equations:

$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{\dot{y}}{\dot{x}} = \dfrac{-\dfrac{a}{m}x + v_0}{\dfrac{a}{m}y}$

Rearranging the terms:

$\dfrac{a}{m}y \, dy = \left(-\dfrac{a}{m}x + v_0\right) dx$

Integrating both sides:

$\dfrac{a}{2m}y^2 = -\dfrac{a}{2m}x^2 + v_0x + C_3$

Since the trajectory passes through the origin ($x = 0, y = 0$), the integration constant $C_3 = 0$. The equation simplifies to:

$\dfrac{a}{2m}x^2 + \dfrac{a}{2m}y^2 - v_0x = 0$

Multiplying the entire equation by $\dfrac{2m}{a}$:

$x^2 + y^2 - \dfrac{2mv_0}{a}x = 0$

Completing the square for the $x$ terms:

$\left(x - \dfrac{mv_0}{a}\right)^2 - \left(\dfrac{mv_0}{a}\right)^2 + y^2 = 0$

$\left(x - \dfrac{mv_0}{a}\right)^2 + y^2 = \left(\dfrac{mv_0}{a}\right)^2$

Answer: (4) $\left(x - \dfrac{m v_0}{a}\right)^2 + y^2 = \left(\dfrac{m v_0}{a}\right)^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Let the length of the rod be $L$. When the rod is rotated by a small angle $\theta$ about the vertical axis passing through its center, the horizontal displacement of its ends is $x = \dfrac{L}{2} \theta$.

The threads of length $\ell$ deviate by an angle $\alpha$, so the horizontal displacement is also given by $x = \ell \sin\alpha \approx \ell \alpha$.

Equating the two expressions for $x$, we get $\dfrac{L}{2} \theta = \ell \alpha \Rightarrow \alpha = \dfrac{L \theta}{2 \ell}$.

The vertical displacement of the rod is $h = \ell(1 - \cos\alpha) \approx \ell \dfrac{\alpha^2}{2}$.

The maximum potential energy of the rod is equal to its total oscillation energy $E$.

$E = mgh = mg \left( \ell \dfrac{\alpha^2}{2} \right) = \dfrac{1}{2} mg\ell \alpha^2$

The restoring torque on the rod is provided by the horizontal component of the tension in the threads. The tension in each thread is $T_0 \approx \dfrac{mg}{2}$.

The horizontal force at each end is $F_H = T_0 \sin\alpha \approx \dfrac{mg}{2} \alpha$.

The restoring torque about the center is $\tau = - 2 F_H \left( \dfrac{L}{2} \right) = - F_H L = - \dfrac{mg}{2} \alpha L$.

Substituting $\alpha = \dfrac{L \theta}{2 \ell}$, we get $\tau = - \dfrac{mg}{2} \left( \dfrac{L \theta}{2 \ell} \right) L = - \dfrac{mgL^2}{4\ell} \theta$.

The moment of inertia of the rod about the central vertical axis is $I = \dfrac{mL^2}{12}$.

Using the equation of motion $I \ddot{\theta} = \tau$, we get:

$\dfrac{mL^2}{12} \ddot{\theta} = - \dfrac{mgL^2}{4\ell} \theta$

$\ddot{\theta} = - \dfrac{3g}{\ell} \theta$

This represents simple harmonic motion with angular frequency $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{3g}{\ell}}$.

The time period of oscillation is $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\ell}{3g}}$.

Answer: (4) $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\ell}{3g}}$, $E = \dfrac{1}{2} m g \ell \alpha^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The moment of inertia of the rod about the horizontal axis passing through its upper end is $I = \dfrac{1}{3} m \ell^2$.

The distance of the center of mass from the pivot is $d = \dfrac{\ell}{2}$.

For small oscillations, the potential energy of the rod at an angle $\theta$ is given by $U = m g d (1 - \cos \theta) \approx m g d \dfrac{\theta^2}{2} = \dfrac{1}{4} m g \ell \theta^2$.

The kinetic energy of the rod is $K = \dfrac{1}{2} I \dot{\theta}^2 = \dfrac{1}{6} m \ell^2 \dot{\theta}^2$.

The total mechanical energy of the system is conserved and can be calculated from the initial conditions $\theta = \theta_0$ and $\dot{\theta} = \dot{\theta}_0$:

$E = K_0 + U_0 = \dfrac{1}{6} m \ell^2 \dot{\theta}_0^2 + \dfrac{1}{4} m g \ell \theta_0^2$

For a simple harmonic oscillator, the mean kinetic energy averaged over one oscillation period is equal to half of the total mechanical energy.

$\langle K \rangle = \dfrac{E}{2} = \dfrac{1}{12} m \ell^2 \dot{\theta}_0^2 + \dfrac{1}{8} m g \ell \theta_0^2$

Answer: (2) $\dfrac{1}{12} m \ell^2 \dot{\theta}_0^2 + \dfrac{1}{8} m g \ell \theta_0^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Let $\theta$ be a small counter-clockwise angular displacement of the pulley from its equilibrium position.

The equation of motion for the deadweight $A$ moving upwards with acceleration $a = R\ddot{\theta}$ is:

$T - m_A g = m_A R\ddot{\theta} \Rightarrow T = m_A(g + R\ddot{\theta})$

The net torque acting on the system of the pulley and the mass $m$ about the axis $O$ is:

$\tau = mgR \sin(\alpha - \theta) - TR$

Applying Newton's second law for rotation, where the moment of inertia of the cylindrical pulley is $\dfrac{1}{2}MR^2$:

$\left(\dfrac{1}{2}MR^2 + mR^2\right)\ddot{\theta} = mgR \sin(\alpha - \theta) - m_A(g + R\ddot{\theta})R$

For small angles, expanding the sine term gives $\sin(\alpha - \theta) \approx \sin\alpha - \theta\cos\alpha$:

$\left(\dfrac{1}{2}MR^2 + mR^2 + m_A R^2\right)\ddot{\theta} = mgR(\sin\alpha - \theta\cos\alpha) - m_A gR$

In the equilibrium position $\theta = 0$, the angular acceleration is zero:

$mgR \sin\alpha - m_A gR = 0 \Rightarrow m_A = m \sin\alpha$

Substituting the value of $m_A$ back into the equation of motion:

$\left(\dfrac{1}{2}M + m + m \sin\alpha\right)R^2 \ddot{\theta} = mgR\sin\alpha - mgR\theta\cos\alpha - (m \sin\alpha) gR$

$\left(\dfrac{M}{2} + m(1 + \sin\alpha)\right)R^2 \ddot{\theta} = -mgR\theta\cos\alpha$

$\ddot{\theta} = -\dfrac{mgR\cos\alpha}{R^2\left(\dfrac{M}{2} + m(1 + \sin\alpha)\right)}\theta$

Comparing this with the standard equation for simple harmonic motion $\ddot{\theta} = -\omega^2\theta$ gives the angular frequency:

$\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{mg\cos\alpha}{R\left(\dfrac{M}{2} + m(1 + \sin\alpha)\right)}}$

Answer: (2)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The equations of motion for the displacements $x_1$, $x_2$, and $x_3$ of the left O atom, C atom, and right O atom respectively are:

$m_O \ddot{x}_1 = -k(x_1 - x_2)$

$m_C \ddot{x}_2 = -k(x_2 - x_1) - k(x_2 - x_3)$

$m_O \ddot{x}_3 = -k(x_3 - x_2)$

For the symmetric mode (1), the two O atoms move in opposite directions with equal amplitude, and the C atom remains stationary. Thus, $x_1 = -x_3 = x$ and $x_2 = 0$.

Substituting these into the first equation gives:

$m_O \ddot{x} = -kx$

The frequency of the symmetric mode is $\omega_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_O}}$.

For the antisymmetric mode (2), the two O atoms move in the same direction with equal amplitude, so $x_1 = x_3 = x$.

Since the center of mass of the system remains stationary:

$m_O x_1 + m_C x_2 + m_O x_3 = 0$

$2m_O x + m_C x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = -\dfrac{2m_O}{m_C} x$

Substituting $x_2$ into the first equation of motion gives:

$m_O \ddot{x} = -k\left(x - \left(-\dfrac{2m_O}{m_C} x\right)\right)$

$m_O \ddot{x} = -k\left(1 + \dfrac{2m_O}{m_C}\right)x$

The frequency of the antisymmetric mode is $\omega_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_O}\left(1 + \dfrac{2m_O}{m_C}\right)}$.

The ratio of the frequency of the antisymmetric mode to the symmetric mode is:

$\dfrac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_O}\left(1 + \dfrac{2m_O}{m_C}\right)}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_O}}} = \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2m_O}{m_C}}$

Answer: (4) $\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2m_O}{m_C}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Let $x$ be the downward displacement of the connector at time $t$.

The velocity of the connector is $v = \dfrac{dx}{dt}$.

The induced emf in the circuit is $e = B \ell v = B \ell \dfrac{dx}{dt}$.

Applying Kirchhoff's loop law for the circuit with inductance $L$ and zero resistance:

$e - L \dfrac{di}{dt} = 0$

$B \ell \dfrac{dx}{dt} = L \dfrac{di}{dt}$

Integrating with initial conditions $x(0) = 0$ and $i(0) = 0$ gives:

$B \ell x = L i \Rightarrow i = \dfrac{B \ell x}{L}$

The upward magnetic force on the connector is $F_m = i \ell B$.

The equation of motion for the connector of mass $m$ is:

$m \dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2} = mg - F_m$

$m \dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2} = mg - i \ell B$

Substituting $i = \dfrac{B \ell x}{L}$:

$m \dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2} = mg - \dfrac{B^2 \ell^2}{L} x$

$\dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \dfrac{B^2 \ell^2}{mL} x = g$

This represents the equation of a simple harmonic oscillator with an external constant force. Let $\omega = \dfrac{B \ell}{\sqrt{mL}}$.

The general solution to this differential equation is:

$x(t) = A \cos(\omega t) + C \sin(\omega t) + \dfrac{g}{\omega^2}$

Using the initial conditions $x(0) = 0$ and $v(0) = 0$:

$x(0) = A + \dfrac{g}{\omega^2} = 0 \Rightarrow A = -\dfrac{g}{\omega^2}$

$v(t) = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = -A \omega \sin(\omega t) + C \omega \cos(\omega t)$

$v(0) = C \omega = 0 \Rightarrow C = 0$

Substituting $A$ and $C$ back into the general solution:

$x(t) = \dfrac{g}{\omega^2} [1 - \cos(\omega t)]$

Substituting $\omega^2 = \dfrac{B^2 \ell^2}{mL}$:

$x(t) = \dfrac{mgL}{B^2 \ell^2} \left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{B \ell}{\sqrt{mL}} t\right)\right]$

Answer: (3) $x(t) = \dfrac{mgL}{B^2 \ell^2} \left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{B \ell}{\sqrt{mL}} t\right)\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Given equation of torsional oscillations is $\varphi = \varphi_0 e^{-\beta t} \cos \omega t$

Differentiating with respect to time $t$, the angular velocity is:

$\dot{\varphi} = \dfrac{d\varphi}{dt} = \varphi_0 [-\beta e^{-\beta t} \cos \omega t - \omega e^{-\beta t} \sin \omega t]$

Substituting $t = 0$:

$\dot{\varphi}(0) = \varphi_0 [-\beta(1)(1) - \omega(1)(0)] = -\beta \varphi_0$

Differentiating again with respect to time $t$, the angular acceleration is:

$\ddot{\varphi} = \dfrac{d\dot{\varphi}}{dt} = -\varphi_0 \dfrac{d}{dt} [e^{-\beta t} (\beta \cos \omega t + \omega \sin \omega t)]$

$\ddot{\varphi} = -\varphi_0 [-\beta e^{-\beta t} (\beta \cos \omega t + \omega \sin \omega t) + e^{-\beta t} (-\beta \omega \sin \omega t + \omega^2 \cos \omega t)]$

Substituting $t = 0$:

$\ddot{\varphi}(0) = -\varphi_0 [-\beta(1)(\beta(1) + 0) + (1)(0 + \omega^2(1))]$

$\ddot{\varphi}(0) = -\varphi_0 [-\beta^2 + \omega^2] = \varphi_0 (\beta^2 - \omega^2)$

Answer: (4) $\dot{\varphi}(0) = -\beta \varphi_0$, $\ddot{\varphi}(0) = \varphi_0 (\beta^2 - \omega^2)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The charge on the capacitor at any time $t$ is given by $q(t) = q_m \cos(\omega_0 t) = C V_m \cos(\omega_0 t)$, where $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$.

The current in the circuit is the rate of decrease of charge on the capacitor, $I(t) = -\dfrac{dq}{dt} = C V_m \omega_0 \sin(\omega_0 t)$.

Substituting $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$, we get $I(t) = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}} \sin(\omega_0 t)$.

The electric energy of the capacitor is $U_E = \dfrac{q^2}{2C} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_m^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t)$.

The magnetic energy of the coil is $U_B = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} L \left( V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}} \sin(\omega_0 t) \right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega_0 t)$.

When the electric energy equals the magnetic energy, $U_E = U_B$, which gives $\cos^2(\omega_0 t) = \sin^2(\omega_0 t)$.

This implies $\cos(\omega_0 t) = \pm \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

The emf of self-inductance in the coil is $\mathcal{E} = -L \dfrac{dI}{dt}$.

Differentiating $I(t)$, we get $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}} \omega_0 \cos(\omega_0 t) = \dfrac{V_m}{L} \cos(\omega_0 t)$.

Thus, $\mathcal{E} = -L \left( \dfrac{V_m}{L} \cos(\omega_0 t) \right) = -V_m \cos(\omega_0 t)$.

Substituting $\cos(\omega_0 t) = \pm \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, we obtain $\mathcal{E} = \pm \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$.

Answer: (3) (a) $I(t) = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}} \sin(\omega_0 t)$; (b) $\mathcal{E} = \pm \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The voltage across the capacitor is given by $V = V_m e^{-\beta t} \cos \omega t$.

The peak values of the modulus of voltage correspond to the points where the voltage curve touches the exponential envelope $V_m e^{-\beta t}$. This occurs when the cosine term is at its maximum magnitude, i.e., $|\cos \omega t| = 1$.

$\omega t = n\pi$

$t_n = \dfrac{n\pi}{\omega}$

The maximum (extremum) values occur when the derivative of the voltage with respect to time is zero.

$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt} (V_m e^{-\beta t} \cos \omega t) = 0$

$V_m e^{-\beta t} (-\beta \cos \omega t - \omega \sin \omega t) = 0$

Since $V_m e^{-\beta t} \neq 0$, we have:

$\beta \cos \omega t + \omega \sin \omega t = 0$

$\tan \omega t = -\dfrac{\beta}{\omega}$

$\omega t = n\pi - \arctan\left(\dfrac{\beta}{\omega}\right)$

$t_n = \dfrac{n\pi - \arctan(\beta/\omega)}{\omega}$

Answer: (4) (a) $t_n = \dfrac{n\pi}{\omega}$, (b) $t_n = \dfrac{n\pi - \arctan(\beta/\omega)}{\omega}$

questions.subject: Physics
options: $I = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R^2} + \omega^2 C^2} \cos(\omega t - \phi)$, where $\tan \phi = \omega R C$
$I = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R^2} + \omega^2 C^2} \cos(\omega t + \phi)$, where $\tan \phi = \omega R C$
questions.solution:
The system can be modeled as an ideal capacitor of capacitance $C$ and an ideal resistor of resistance $R$ connected in parallel, since the poorly conducting medium fills the interelectrode space and experiences the same potential difference as the capacitor.

The alternating voltage applied across the combination is given by:
$V = V_m \cos \omega t$

The conduction current flowing through the active resistance of the medium is:
$I_R = \dfrac{V}{R} = \dfrac{V_m}{R} \cos \omega t$

The displacement current (or the current charging the capacitor) is:
$I_C = \dfrac{dq}{dt} = C \dfrac{dV}{dt} = C \dfrac{d}{dt}(V_m \cos \omega t) = -V_m \omega C \sin \omega t$

The total steady-state current $I$ in the lead wires is the sum of the conduction and displacement currents:
$I = I_R + I_C = \dfrac{V_m}{R} \cos \omega t - V_m \omega C \sin \omega t$

To express this in the form of a single cosine function, we multiply and divide by $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R^2} + \omega^2 C^2}$:
$I = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R^2} + \omega^2 C^2} \left( \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{R}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R^2} + \omega^2 C^2}} \cos \omega t - \dfrac{\omega C}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R^2} + \omega^2 C^2}} \sin \omega t \right)$

Let $\cos \phi = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{R}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R^2} + \omega^2 C^2}}$ and $\sin \phi = \dfrac{\omega C}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R^2} + \omega^2 C^2}}$.

This gives:
$\tan \phi = \dfrac{\sin \phi}{\cos \phi} = \omega R C$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(\omega t + \phi) = \cos \omega t \cos \phi - \sin \omega t \sin \phi$, the total current becomes:
$I = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R^2} + \omega^2 C^2} \cos(\omega t + \phi)$

Answer: (3) $I = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{R^2} + \omega^2 C^2} \cos(\omega t + \phi)$, where $\tan \phi = \omega R C$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The current in the circuit is given by:

$I = \dfrac{V_1}{R} = \dfrac{80}{160} = 0.5$ A

Let the active resistance of the coil be $r$ and its inductance be $L$. The voltage across the coil is $V_2$, which has a resistive component $V_r = I r$ and an inductive component $V_L = I X_L$.

The total voltage $V$ of the circuit is the vector sum of the voltage across $R$ and the voltage across the coil. Since $V_1$ and $V_r$ are in phase, we have:

$V^2 = (V_1 + V_r)^2 + V_L^2$

$V^2 = V_1^2 + 2 V_1 V_r + V_r^2 + V_L^2$

Since $V_2^2 = V_r^2 + V_L^2$, we can write:

$V^2 = V_1^2 + V_2^2 + 2 V_1 V_r$

Substituting the given values:

$220^2 = 80^2 + 180^2 + 2(80)V_r$

$48400 = 6400 + 32400 + 160 V_r$

$48400 = 38800 + 160 V_r$

$160 V_r = 9600$

$V_r = 60$ V

The heat power generated in the coil is due to its active resistance $r$:

$P = I^2 r = I V_r$

$P = 0.5 \times 60 = 30$ W

Answer: $30$

questions.subject: Physics
options: The mean mechanical power developed by external forces to maintain rotation is $P = \dfrac{\omega^2 \Phi_0^2 R}{2(R^2 + \omega^2 L^2)}$
questions.solution:
Given magnetic flux $\Phi = \Phi_0 \cos \omega t$

Induced emf $\mathcal{E} = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = \Phi_0 \omega \sin \omega t$

The circuit has resistance $R$ and inductance $L$, so the impedance is $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2}$

The amplitude of the induced current is $I_m = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}_0}{Z} = \dfrac{\Phi_0 \omega}{\sqrt{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2}}$

The current lags the emf by a phase angle $\varphi$, given by $\tan \varphi = \dfrac{\omega L}{R}$

The mean mechanical power developed by external forces equals the mean electrical power dissipated as heat in the resistance.

$P = \langle I^2 R \rangle = \dfrac{I_m^2 R}{2} = \dfrac{\omega^2 \Phi_0^2 R}{2(R^2 + \omega^2 L^2)}$

Answer: (1, 2, 3)

questions.subject: Physics
options: It represents the reciprocal of the wave velocity.
It represents the angular frequency of the wave.
questions.solution:
The general solution of a one-dimensional wave equation is of the form $f(x \pm vt)$ or $f(t \pm \dfrac{x}{v})$, where $v$ is the wave velocity.

The given function is $f(t + \alpha x)$.

Comparing the argument $t + \alpha x$ with the standard form $t + \dfrac{x}{v}$, we get:

$\alpha = \dfrac{1}{v}$

Thus, the constant $\alpha$ represents the reciprocal of the wave velocity.

Answer: (2) It represents the reciprocal of the wave velocity.

questions.subject: Physics
options: For a longitudinal wave, the density of the medium is maximum at points where the strain $\dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x}$ is most negative.
The strain $\dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x}$ at $t=0$ is given by $ak \sin(kx)$.
The particle velocity $v$ at $t=0$ is given by $v = a\omega \sin(kx)$.
At points where $\xi = 0$ and $\dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} < 0$, the particle velocity is in the positive $x$-direction.
questions.solution:
The given equation of the plane wave is $\xi = a \cos(\omega t - kx)$.

The particle velocity $v$ is given by the partial derivative of displacement with respect to time:

$v = \dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial t} = -a\omega \sin(\omega t - kx)$

At $t = 0$, the particle velocity is:

$v = -a\omega \sin(-kx) = a\omega \sin(kx)$

Thus, statement (3) is correct.

The strain in the medium is given by the partial derivative of displacement with respect to position:

$\dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} = ak \sin(\omega t - kx)$

At $t = 0$, the strain is:

$\dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} = ak \sin(-kx) = -ak \sin(kx)$

Thus, statement (2) is incorrect.

For a longitudinal wave, the density $\rho$ of the medium is related to the strain by the equation:

$\rho = \dfrac{\rho_0}{1 + \dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x}}$

The density $\rho$ is maximum when the denominator $1 + \dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x}$ is minimum. This occurs when the strain $\dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x}$ is most negative (maximum compression).

Thus, statement (1) is correct.

At points where $\xi = 0$, we have $\cos(\omega t - kx) = 0$, which implies $\sin(\omega t - kx) = \pm 1$.

Given that the strain $\dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} < 0$, we have:

$ak \sin(\omega t - kx) < 0 \Rightarrow \sin(\omega t - kx) = -1$

Substituting this into the velocity equation, we get:

$v = -a\omega (-1) = a\omega$

Since $a\omega > 0$, the particle velocity is in the positive $x$-direction.

Thus, statement (4) is correct.

Answer: (1, 3, 4)

questions.subject: Physics
options: At $t = 0$, the strain $\dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x}$ is given by $-ak \sin kx$.
In the case of longitudinal oscillations, the density of the medium at $t = 0$ is maximum at $x = \dfrac{\pi}{2k}, \dfrac{5\pi}{2k}, \dots$
At $t = \dfrac{T}{4}$, the magnitude of the velocity of the particles is maximum at the nodes.
At $t = \dfrac{T}{4}$, the magnitude of the velocity of the particles is maximum at the antinodes.
questions.solution:
The given equation of the standing wave is $\xi = a \cos kx \cos \omega t$.

The strain in the medium is given by the spatial derivative of the displacement:

$\dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} = -ak \sin kx \cos \omega t$

At $t = 0$, $\cos \omega t = \cos 0 = 1$, so the strain is $\dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} = -ak \sin kx$. This makes option (1) correct.

For longitudinal waves, the density of the medium is related to the strain by the continuity equation, given as $\rho = \rho_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial x}\right)$.

At $t = 0$, substituting the strain gives the density as $\rho = \rho_0 (1 + ak \sin kx)$.

The density is maximum when $\sin kx = 1$, which occurs at:

$kx = \dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{5\pi}{2}, \dots$

$x = \dfrac{\pi}{2k}, \dfrac{5\pi}{2k}, \dots$

This makes option (2) correct.

The velocity of the particles is given by the time derivative of the displacement:

$v = \dfrac{\partial \xi}{\partial t} = -a\omega \cos kx \sin \omega t$

At $t = \dfrac{T}{4}$, the phase is $\omega t = \dfrac{2\pi}{T} \cdot \dfrac{T}{4} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$, so $\sin \omega t = 1$.

The velocity at this instant becomes $v = -a\omega \cos kx$.

The magnitude of the velocity is $|v| = a\omega |\cos kx|$. This magnitude is maximum when $|\cos kx| = 1$, which corresponds to the positions of the antinodes of the standing wave. This makes option (4) correct and option (3) incorrect.

Answer: (1, 2, 4)
